name: Integration patterns (Luxair)
description: SOA vs microservices, ESB trade-offs, sync vs async fan-out and legacy integration migration — from the Luxair interview prep docs.

topics.name: SOA & ESB
topics.description: Service-oriented architecture and enterprise service buses; central integration layers and their risks.
topics.studyNotes:
SOA and microservices share the idea of services; they differ in where integration logic lives. SOA centralizes it — an ESB (enterprise service bus) carries routing, transformation and orchestration, with shared contracts governed centrally. Microservices decentralize: smart endpoints, dumb pipes — the logic lives in the services, the transport stays thin.

Why an ESB becomes a bottleneck: business logic accumulates in the bus; every change to any flow funnels through one platform and often one team; deployments and scaling couple everything that touches the bus. The bus becomes the organization's critical path — a Conway's Law artifact: a central integration team produces a central integration layer.

The balanced take: SOA's ideas survive — contracts, reuse, governance — and central integration still fits some enterprises (many legacy systems, strong compliance). The modern default, though, is decentralized ownership with events for decoupling, and the bus reserved for what genuinely must be central.

## Composition vocabulary

- Orchestration vs choreography: orchestration has a central brain calling the steps (a saga orchestrator); choreography has services reacting to each other's events with no central coordinator. Choreography decouples but makes the overall flow harder to see — observability becomes the price of freedom.
- Aggregator: one service fans out to several sources and composes a single response — common at the API edge (backend-for-frontend) so clients avoid five round trips.
- Coarse-grained vs fine-grained services: coarse-grained interfaces exchange bigger, complete business payloads in fewer calls — often better across network boundaries than chatty fine-grained calls.

topics.name: Integration migration
topics.description: Safely migrating flows off a legacy integration platform (e.g. Mule/ESB) with behavior compatibility.
topics.studyNotes:
Migrating flows off a legacy integration platform (Mule/ESB-style) without breaking consumers is a strangler exercise, not a rewrite.

The method: route ONE flow at a time to the new implementation BEHIND THE SAME CONTRACT — consumers keep calling the same interface and cannot tell the difference. Verify behaviour compatibility empirically, not by reading code: shadow/parallel runs comparing old vs new outputs, contract tests, replaying real recorded traffic. Only after the outputs match does the flow cut over.

Order by risk: low-risk flows first to harden the process, critical flows last. Keep a fast way back — routing back to the legacy flow must be a switch, not a project — and monitor error rates per migrated flow so a regression is visible immediately.

The dirty secret of such migrations: legacy flows contain undocumented transformations. Discovery is empirical — capture real inputs and outputs, diff against the new implementation, and treat every mismatch as either a bug to fix or a behaviour to consciously preserve.

topics.name: Async fan-out
topics.description: Notifying many systems after a business event: critical path vs asynchronous distribution.
topics.studyNotes:
A booking is created and five systems must know. The design question: what does the USER have to wait for? Only that stays synchronous — persist the booking, answer. Notifications are not on the critical path.

The shape: publish ONE booking-created event to a broker; the five consumers subscribe independently. The producer does not call five APIs — it does not even know how many consumers exist. Adding a sixth system is a subscription, not a code change.

Failure isolation is the real win: each consumer has its own retries with backoff and its own DLQ; one failing system does not block the other four; duplicates are handled idempotently (at-least-once delivery).

The exception that proves the rule: if a step must gate the outcome — fraud check before confirmation — it belongs on the synchronous critical path by definition. Sync vs async is decided by the business dependency, not by fashion.

Why Node.js keeps appearing in this space: integration services are I/O-bound — many concurrent network calls — which a non-blocking event loop handles cheaply. Its weakness is CPU-bound work (one heavy computation stalls every request); mitigations are worker threads, offloading to queues/services, or a different runtime for compute-heavy paths.

topics.name: Capacity Sizing & Back-of-Envelope Math
topics.description: Turning '100M requests/month' into rps, concurrency, and infrastructure questions; I/O vs CPU bound.
topics.studyNotes:
"The system gets 100 million requests a month" — the interview wants you to turn that into engineering numbers on the spot.

The arithmetic: 100M/month ≈ 100M / (30 × 86,400s) ≈ 39 requests per second AVERAGE. The average is the beginning, not the answer: sizing needs the PEAK (daily and seasonal patterns can put peak at 5–10× average), the cost of a request (a cache hit and a 5-table join are different beasts), and the downstream limits — the DB and external APIs cap you long before the app tier does.

Concurrency — how many requests are in flight at once — has a rough formula worth saying aloud: concurrency ≈ rps × latency in seconds. 39 rps at 200ms ≈ 8 concurrent requests — tiny; 39 rps at 3s (slow downstream) ≈ 120 in flight — connection pools and memory suddenly matter. This is why latency degradation cascades into resource exhaustion.

Always answer with percentiles: average latency hides the tail; p95/p99 tell you what your slowest users experience and what you should size and alert on.

I/O-bound vs CPU-bound decides the runtime story: integration/API workloads are I/O-bound (DB, APIs, files, Redis, queues) — a non-blocking event loop (Node.js) handles many concurrent waits cheaply. CPU-bound work — hashing, image/video, huge JSON parsing, heavy loops — blocks the event loop and stalls everyone; worker threads or a separate service fix that.

Runtime packaging footnote: VMs virtualize hardware with a full OS each (strong isolation, slow to boot, heavy); containers share the host kernel (light, fast, dense) — which is why the container + Kubernetes combination won the deployment story.

questions.title: SOA vs microservices, and the ESB bottleneck
questions.prompt: What is the difference between SOA and microservices — and why can an ESB become a bottleneck?
questions.answerStructureHint: Shared bus and smart pipes vs smart endpoints and dumb pipes; central logic accumulation, coupling and single point of failure.

expectedPoints.label: Core architectural difference
expectedPoints.description: SOA centralizes integration logic (ESB, shared contracts); microservices favor decentralized ownership — smart endpoints, dumb pipes.

expectedPoints.label: Why the ESB becomes a bottleneck
expectedPoints.description: Business logic accumulates in the bus; every change funnels through one team/platform; scaling and deploys couple everything.

expectedPoints.label: Balanced, not dogmatic
expectedPoints.description: Notes SOA ideas live on (contracts, reuse) and central integration still fits some enterprises.

followUps.prompt: How does Conway's Law show up in this choice?

questions.title: Notify five systems after a booking
questions.prompt: After a booking is created, five other systems must be notified. Sync or async — how would you design it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Keep the critical path minimal, publish one event and fan out async; handle per-consumer failure, ordering and duplicates.

expectedPoints.label: Minimal critical path
expectedPoints.description: Only what the user must wait for stays synchronous (persist booking); notifications are not on the critical path.
acceptedSignals: five sequential calls would be slow

expectedPoints.label: Publish once, fan out via broker
expectedPoints.description: Emit one booking-created event; consumers subscribe independently instead of the producer calling five APIs.

expectedPoints.label: Per-consumer failure isolation
expectedPoints.description: Each consumer has its own retries/DLQ; one failing system doesn't block the others; duplicates handled idempotently.

followUps.prompt: Which of the five would you still call synchronously if, say, fraud check must pass before confirming?

questions.title: Migrating off a legacy integration platform
questions.prompt: You must migrate integration flows from a legacy platform (like Mule/ESB) to plain services. How do you do it without breaking consumers?
questions.answerStructureHint: Inventory and characterize flows, migrate incrementally behind stable contracts, verify behavior parity (shadow traffic), keep rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Incremental, flow by flow
expectedPoints.description: Strangler-style: route one flow at a time to the new implementation behind the same contract, not a big-bang rewrite.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies behavior compatibility
expectedPoints.description: Compares old vs new outputs — shadow/parallel runs, contract tests, replaying real traffic — before cutover.

expectedPoints.label: Risk ordering and rollback
expectedPoints.description: Start with low-risk flows, keep a fast way back, monitor error rates per migrated flow.

followUps.prompt: Legacy ESB flows often contain undocumented transformations. How do you discover what the flow really does?

questions.title: Node.js for integration services
questions.prompt: Why is Node.js a good fit for integration/API services, and where is it a poor choice?
questions.answerStructureHint: Event loop excels at I/O-bound concurrent calls; single thread suffers on CPU-bound work; mitigation via workers.

expectedPoints.label: Strong for I/O-bound work
expectedPoints.description: Non-blocking event loop handles many concurrent network calls cheaply — exactly what integration services do.

expectedPoints.label: Weak for CPU-bound work
expectedPoints.description: A single-threaded loop blocks on heavy computation, stalling every request.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the mitigations
expectedPoints.description: Worker threads, offloading to services/queues, or picking another runtime for compute-heavy paths.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect event-loop blocking in a production Node.js service?

questions.title: 100M requests a month — now what?
questions.prompt: A service is expected to take 100 million requests a month. Walk me through the back-of-envelope sizing and the questions you would ask.

expectedPoints.label: Does the arithmetic
expectedPoints.description: ≈39 rps average; states the formula and computes.

expectedPoints.label: Average vs peak + request cost
expectedPoints.description: Peak can be many times average; cache hit vs heavy query; downstream/DB limits bound the system.

expectedPoints.label: Concurrency formula
expectedPoints.description: In-flight ≈ rps × latency seconds; slow downstream multiplies concurrency and eats pools.

expectedPoints.label: Sizes on percentiles
expectedPoints.description: p95/p99 over averages for sizing and alerting.
